answer:
Let \( p \) be a prime number. We will show that \( 8p^2 +1 \) cannot be prime for \( p \neq 3 \).

First, we consider the case when \( p \equiv 1 \pmod{3} \). In this case, \( 8p^2+1 \) is of the form \( 3k+1 \) or \( 3k+2 \).

If \( 8p^2+1 \) is of the form \( 3k+1 \), then \( 8p^2 \equiv 0 \pmod{3} \), which implies \( p^2 \equiv 1 \pmod{3} \). Since \( p \) is a prime congruent to \( 1 \pmod{3} \), it follows that \( p \) is of the form \( 3k+1 \). In this case, \( p \) and \( 8p^2+1 \) are not both prime, as \( p \) is a prime of the form \( 3k+1 \) and \( 8p^2+1 \) is of the form \( 3(2p^2+1) \), which is not prime for any integer \( p \).

If \( 8p^2+1 \) is of the form \( 3k+2 \), then \( 8p^2 \equiv 2 \pmod{3} \), which implies \( p^2 \equiv 2 \pmod{3} \). However, there is no prime number of the form \( 2 \pmod{3} \) that is greater than \( 2 \).

Now, we consider the case when \( p \equiv 2 \pmod{3} \). In this case, \( 8p^2+1 \) is of the form \( 3k \). Since \( p \) is even, it follows that \( p \) is not prime. Therefore, \( p \) and \( 8p^2+1 \) cannot be both prime.

Thus, the only case where \( p \) and \( 8p^2+1 \) can be prime simultaneously is when \( p=3 \).

\boxed{3}
